intro: Thanks for taking a look at my portfolio! Many of these projects are from my last job, so I’m limited to how much detail I can provide. Nonetheless, this portfolio shows the projects that I’m most proud of, and that showed my best skills. Enjoy! -Joshua Kopaunik
recommendation:
Joshua is a talented and dedicated engineer with a real passion for pushing technological boundaries, particularly in the realm of AI and machine learning. He consistently impressed me with his strong technical abilities in AI/ML, staying current with the latest advancements and effectively sharing his learnings with the team. Joshua is a good and careful coder, consistently producing well-structured, thoroughly documented, and thoughtfully designed code.

During his tenure, Joshua made significant contributions to several key projects. He was instrumental in the development of our AI Smart Search customer-facing tool, where he designed and implemented core backend functionalities, integrated AI commands, and set up the datastore. Additionally, Joshua created an internal tool for our content team to be able to populate our AI bots with internal knowledge, including handling how that information would be chunked, vectorized, and retrieved. He implemented a robust testing system for it. Joshua was always on the cutting-edge of RAG research, and how best to effectively retrieve and rank embeddings. Joshua also demonstrated strong collaborative skills, working effectively with frontend developers and other team members to bring these projects to fruition.

Joshua is a quick learner, readily adapting to new technologies and frameworks, such as Google Cloud Platform and various AI models, and applying this knowledge to improve project implementations. He consistently embraced daily challenges and contributed to spirited, productive discourse within our team.

Joshua leaves behind a legacy of strong technical contributions and a forward-thinking approach. He would be a significant asset to any organization seeking a software engineer with robust AI/ML skills, a commitment to quality, and a collaborative spirit. I highly recommend Joshua.

Rogan Griffin | Chief Innovation Officer
Email: rgriffin@interplaylearning.com
Phone: (650) 646 - 2084

projects.title: SAM
projects.purpose: SAM was already an existing product when I joined IL. However, one of my first projects was to improve SAM’s personality, usefulness, and reliability.
projects.details: This improvement came in two parts. The first part was prompt engineering. This included giving SAM better context of the app it was built in, which dramatically improved its usefulness and relevance. The second improvement came in the form of API parameter tuning. Specifically, reducing the temperature so that it was more predictable and consistent.
projects.responsibilities: Led improvements, handled both prompt and parameter engineering.

projects.title: The Embeddings Editor
projects.purpose: Our award winning SAM system was fairly basic at the start. Just an LLM plugged into the UI with basic state information. The content team wanted a tool to create custom embeddings based on our existing content, in order to improve retrieval performance and grounding quality. This would be used for future systems, like an update to SAM.
projects.details: This was an internal web-app, where the content team could create namespaces and documents. It was built with HTML, Javascript/Jquery, and CSS, and managed deploying new embeddings to production. It also contained an interface for testing these new embeddings,enabling the content team to test their new embeddings to ensure they were being retrieved as expected.
projects.responsibilities: I implemented the wireframe and built the deployment and testing logic. Additionally, I also taught a lesson to non-technical staff on what embeddings were, how they worked, and to suggest best practices for them going forward.

projects.title: Charles, the ama bot
projects.purpose: The company is large enough that knowledge is scattered across the entire organization. As such, there was an internal process for asking the whole company a question, particularly used by sales-people and the customer success team to help clients. There was an internal bot that would make a first attempt at answering the question based on previous answers and documentation.
projects.details: Charles was already built when I joined Interplay Learning. However, its performance was unideal and needed improvement. I was tasked with improving Charles RAG as well as its general performance. In the end, I split the RAG into several steps which ultimately resulted in much higher quality results. One of the major issues that I found was that many questions actually had several questions, which resulted in diluted and vague query embeddings. One of the major improvements came by having an LLM first split the query into an array of individual questions, then running a dedicated retrieval on each of those questions, and only then passed that data to an answering LLM. This enabled that final LLM to have documents more closely related to the questions being asked.
projects.responsibilities: Researched and implemented multi-step retrieval and answering flow. Improved overall bot performance.

projects.title: Jira streamlining
projects.purpose: The company wants Agentic capabilities to track and manage development. We use Jira internally, so I was tasked with creating agentic systems to manage this.
projects.details: This started by translating existing Jira integration code into something the AgentsSDK could utilize. When I was nearing finishing that Jira Agent, I was instructed to pivot the Jira integration into an MCP server.
projects.responsibilities: On top of implementing the Agent and MCP server, I also developed the standard and process for internal MCP deployments. This had a focus on ease of deployment, scalability, and cost savings in mind. Once I created that standard, I also created thorough documentation for future MCP deployments.

projects.title: Embedding Model Migration
projects.purpose: In January, 2024, OpenAI released the 3rd generation of their Embedding models. As part of my role at Interplay Learning, I caught this development and eventually migrated the internal RAG stack over to the new system.
projects.details: These new models had a different dimension then the old ones, so I had to create a new Pinecone index and then populate it with regenerated embeddings. I also had to engineer the embedding text generation to ensure quality matching. In this process, I created a codebase that could recursively scrape our own content, generate embeddings for a particular model, then upload them to a pinecone namespace. It was built to be flexible, and proved useful for several future projects.
projects.responsibilities: This development came about due to my responsibility for keeping the company up-to-date on AI technologies. I took the initiative to experiment with the new models, then recommend a transition based on those experiments. I then was granted resources to implement that transition.

projects.title: Quiz Generator
projects.purpose: The Product team wanted to explore a product where account admins (e.g., human instructors) could create their own quizzes, to better target their specific learners based on the in-person courses. To accelerate that, the Product team wanted to be able to tie Generative AI into the app to generate a first-draft of the quiz.
projects.details: The admins could load a specific course or specific quiz to start from. Then, they could either manually add/edit questions or open an AI chat interface and request changes, new questions, or complete rewrites. The AI would then generate and update the quiz accordingly.
projects.responsibilities: I was given a v0.1 prototype, and instructed to make substantial improvements and changes, including implementing the GenAI feature. This meant a web-app that would load in a certain lesson, course, or quiz, then allow the user to manually make changes and/or instruct AI to make changes.

experiments.title: Smart Search Beta
experiments.hypothesis: Hypothesis: Junior tradespeople can end up making many calls to their leadership. If we provide an AI powered search engine for our vast content library, we can reduce the amount of time senior tradespeople spend on calls, and improve the hands-on learning and confidence of our users.
experiments.details: Technical details:This was a collaboration between Innovation, Product, and UX. This project spanned for over a year, and I was the sole innovation engineer assigned to it. It went through several versions of UX design, several separate prototypes, and culminated in a private beta for real users.
experiments.results: Responsibilities: For most iterations, I was the sole developer, and worked with Product and UX to explore capabilities and refine the use case for this GenAI app. For that last beta, I helped onboard a front-end contractor to speed up development. I led the development and architected the API, backend, and custom Agentic tech stack (this was prior to the Agents SDK).

experiments.title: Attention viewer
experiments.hypothesis: Hypothesis: If we can view the values of the attention mechanisms within a transformer, then we can use that information to debug prompts, and create better prompts.
experiments.details: Technical details: This utilized a lot of open source resources. Initially, I used BertViz. However, Bert does not utilize masked attention, but rather unmasked. However, it is believed that SoTA transformers do use masked attention. Thus, I switched over to using Hugging Face’s Transformer library to run inferences of LLama, then visualized that attention instance using HTML. There were many configuration options, such as picking specific layers, heads, how one particular token is paying attention, which tokens have the most attention on them, and more.
experiments.results:
Responsibilities: This was one of my passion projects. It entailed researching and learning about the inner structure of an LLM, then proposing this system for development, before finally building it.
Results: It ended up not being super useful. Inference was slow due to us not having high end GPUs, and outputs were too abstract to gain any value from. Nonetheless, it was a very beneficial tool to build as it taught me a lot about how these models work.

experiments.title: Advanced RAG
experiments.hypothesis: Purpose description:  Interplay Learning’s RAG is quite simple. It was simple to implement. However, I felt that it was time to go from simple nearest-k retrieval in order to improve our currently B-tier results.
experiments.details: Technical details: I explored Ensemble RAG, Cohere reranking, different embedding models, and more to see how these compare. This meant vibe coding (since it was a quick smoke test) various implementations and data visualization techniques.
experiments.results: There were some very intriguing insights, but this was what I was working on when my department got dissolved, which means I wasn’t able to fully explore all my ideas or the data I had already created.

experiments.title: Context pre-stuffing
experiments.hypothesis: Hypothesis: LLMs determine their behavior, in part, from the context already provided. If we stuff the previous context with 100% factual responses then ask a new question, we can increase the accuracy of our systems and reduce hallucinations.
experiments.details: Technical details: We used an open source, QA-type benchmark as our dataset. The control data was generated by taking GPT-3.5 Turbo and having it answer n-number of questions. Then, we took 3 separate instances of GPT-3.5 Turbo, each with a different context full of factual answers, and had them each answer the exact same set of questions. The correctness of this answer was graded by a separate LLM instance.
experiments.results: Results: The hypothesis proved partially true. We did increase the amount of correct answers recalled slightly. However, we also dramatically increased hallucinations. This increase in hallucinations was due to me not including “I don’t know” answers in the pre-stuffing, resulting in the model always making a guess attempt. I performed a follow up experiment, where I included “I don’t know” answers (based on questions that it got wrong before), and the increases in truthful answers and hallucinations vanished. I don’t have all the result numbers anymore. However, I do distinctly remember having statistical significance ( p < 0.005). It was an intriguing result, but proved to not be a method to improve truthfulness in LLMs.
